questions.q: In primary-backup replication, when does the primary acknowledge a write to the client?
options: After the backup has durably stored the write
Immediately after the primary writes to its own storage, before contacting the backup
Only after all nodes have written to their local storage
When the client explicitly requests confirmation with a separate message
questions.explanation: Strong primary-backup replication requires the backup to acknowledge before the primary replies to the client, ensuring durability. Option 1 would sacrifice durability. Option 2 is inconsistent with the primary-backup model.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of quorum-based replication over primary-backup replication?
options: Any available quorum of nodes can serve reads and handle failures without requiring a specific primary role
Quorum replication guarantees that all nodes have identical data at all times
Quorum replication eliminates the need for any consensus protocol
Quorum replication reduces the number of network messages by half
questions.explanation: Quorum replication allows multiple nodes to serve as coordinators and tolerates failures more flexibly than primary-backup, which depends on a single primary. Option 1 is false; quorum doesn't ensure uniform state. Option 2 is a misconception; quorum replication still requires coordination.

questions.q: In eventual consistency, what guarantee can an application expect?
options: If no new writes occur, all replicas will eventually have the same data
All replicas will always have identical data within 100 milliseconds
Writes are immediately visible to all readers globally
Read operations never return stale data
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency promises convergence only when writes stop and all updates propagate. Option 1 assumes real-time consistency, which eventual consistency doesn't provide. Option 2 guarantees immediate visibility, violating the definition.

questions.q: What is a conflict in multi-master replication and how is it typically resolved?
options: Two masters apply different writes to the same data; resolution uses timestamps, version vectors, or application logic
A conflict occurs when two nodes have different network latencies
Conflicts are eliminated by ensuring only one master ever writes data
A conflict means one master is completely unreachable and cannot be recovered
questions.explanation: Conflicts in multi-master replication arise from concurrent writes to the same data and require conflict resolution strategies. Option 1 is a network property, not a data conflict. Option 2 describes single-master systems, not multi-master.

questions.q: What does 'durability' mean in the context of replication?
options: Data persisted to non-volatile storage survives node failures and is not lost
Replicas can be updated without requiring any network communication
The system guarantees that reads never see uncommitted writes
Replicated data is automatically compressed to save storage space
questions.explanation: Durability means committed data persists across failures. Option 1 is a network property. Option 2 confuses durability with consistency. Option 3 is irrelevant to durability.

questions.q: In the read-repair technique used in eventual consistency, what happens?
options: When a stale value is read, it is detected and updated to the latest version before being returned
Reads are temporarily blocked while all replicas reach consensus on the latest version
Stale data is deleted from the system without returning it to the client
The client is responsible for merging different versions it receives from different replicas
questions.explanation: Read repair detects and corrects stale data on read operations, improving consistency. Option 1 is too strict and would block reads. Option 2 implies deletion without client notification. Option 3 places the burden on the client.

questions.q: What is the key difference between synchronous and asynchronous replication?
options: Synchronous replication waits for backup acknowledgment before returning to the client; asynchronous does not
Asynchronous replication is faster but may lose recent writes on primary failure; synchronous is slower but durable
Synchronous replication does not use network communication between replicas
Asynchronous replication guarantees lower latency to clients while maintaining stronger consistency
questions.explanation: Synchronous replication provides stronger durability at the cost of latency; asynchronous replication improves performance but risks data loss. Option 1 correctly identifies the tradeoff. Option 2 is false; asynchronous replication still uses networks.

questions.q: In vector clocks, what information does each entry represent?
options: The logical clock value for a specific process, used to detect causality and concurrent events
The physical time at which a message was sent between two nodes
The number of bytes transmitted by each node in the system
The priority level assigned to each replica in the replication group
questions.explanation: Vector clocks track the logical clock of each process to determine causal ordering and identify concurrent events. Option 1 measures physical time, not logical causality. Option 2 tracks data volume, not event ordering.

questions.q: What is the 'write quorum' in quorum-based replication?
options: The minimum number of replicas that must acknowledge a write before it is considered committed
The number of replicas required to handle read operations
The total number of replicas minus one that must fail before writes are blocked
A group of replicas elected to filter duplicate write requests
questions.explanation: The write quorum ensures a minimum number of replicas acknowledge a write for safety. Option 1 applies to read operations, not writes. Option 2 describes failure tolerance, not write commitment.

questions.q: In replication, what is a 'ghost write'?
options: A write applied by one replica that is overwritten before reaching other replicas, causing data divergence
A write operation that completes successfully but the acknowledgment is lost in the network
A write that targets a replica that is currently offline
A write operation issued by a client that was never received by any replica
questions.explanation: A ghost write occurs when one replica's write diverges from others before propagation, leading to consistency issues. Option 1 describes network loss, not divergence. Option 2 assumes offline replicas ignore writes. Option 3 is a lost message, not a ghost write.
